content.text: Bridge Call and War Room Setup Protocols

The first five minutes of a declared P0 or P1 incident are the most important for coordination. A bridge call that takes ten minutes to set up, a Slack channel where the wrong people are posting, or an exec who finds out about the incident two hours in through a customer complaint -- each of these is a process failure that compounds the technical one. The protocols below make the setup deterministic: any IC on any rotation can spin up a functioning war room within three minutes.

A war room is not just a meeting. It is a structured information environment with clear ownership of each communication channel, defined escalation paths, and agreed norms for who may post what. Without that structure, a P0 bridge devolves into fifteen engineers asking each other for status updates while the IC loses the thread.

attrs.alt: War room channel structure: central bridge channel (#inc-id) feeds three sub-channels: tech (SRE and engineering investigation), comms (status updates), and exec (P0/P1 briefings). IC reads all three and delegates to channel leads.

content.text: Every declared incident of P2 or higher creates at least two Slack channels. P0 and P1 incidents create three. The IC bot provisions these automatically when the IC runs the declare command.
IC, Tech Lead, Comms Lead, oncall SRE, Scribe
Primary bridge: all decisions, status, escalation
SRE, engineers, SMEs pulled in by Tech Lead
Investigation details, log snippets, graphs; not for status updates
Comms Lead, Support, PM, Customer Success
Customer-facing draft updates; do not post raw logs here
IC, VP Engineering, Legal/Privacy (P0 only), Exec Sponsor
Periodic IC briefings; no technical detail unless requested

The IC is the only person who reads all four channels. The Tech Lead owns #inc-{id}-tech and summarises findings back to the bridge channel. The Comms Lead owns #inc-{id}-comms and posts approved status updates to the external status page. The Scribe records timeline entries in the bridge channel.
Declaring the Incident and Provisioning the War Room

The IC bot automates channel creation, permission setting, and initial notifications. The IC should not create channels manually -- manual channels are inconsistently named, lack the correct topic and permissions, and are not linked to the incident record.

# In any Slack channel:
/ic declare --severity p1 --title "Quality regression claude-3-5-haiku us-east-1"

# Bot response:
# Incident INC-20240719-004 declared at P1
# Bridge:  #inc-20240719-004         (you have been added as IC)
# Tech:    #inc-20240719-004-tech
# Comms:   #inc-20240719-004-comms
# Status page: draft update created (not yet published)
# PagerDuty: P1 routing applied; oncall SRE paged
# Postmortem: draft created at notion.so/incidents/INC-20240719-004
After the bot creates the channels, the IC completes the bootstrap within the first two minutes:
in the bridge channel. Bot pins the incident title, severity, declared time, IC name, and links to the postmortem draft.
. IC updates this if severity changes.
. Bot adds them to the bridge and tech channels.
. Bot adds them to bridge and comms channels.
IC writes the first status update in the bridge channel using the status template (see below).
. Bot posts a Zoom link pinned to the bridge channel and starts recording.
A bridge call with no norms collapses into crosstalk. The following norms apply to all P0 and P1 bridge calls and should be stated by the IC at the start of every call.

This is the incident bridge for INC-{id}. I am {IC name}, Incident Commander. Current severity is {P0/P1}. The Technical Lead is {name}. If you are on this call and do not have a specific assigned task, please mute and observe. All investigation findings go in #inc-{id}-tech. Customer communications go through Comms Lead {name} only. I will give a status update every ten minutes. If you need to raise something urgent, type it in the bridge Slack channel or address me directly by name on the call.
The IC repeats the channel routing rule at every update interval until the bridge has been running for at least 30 minutes. People join late and miss the opening.
Background noise prevents the IC from hearing critical updates
IC names violators once; second violation gets a mute from the moderator
Creates information asymmetry; IC misses context
IC redirects to bridge Slack channel
Norm only; IC models the behaviour
Oral-only findings are lost when the call ends
IC redirects: 'post that in the tech channel'
Bridge calls often have 8-15 people; overlap prevents clear decisions
IC moderates by calling on participants
No speculation on root cause on call
Premature theories spread faster than evidence and waste investigation time
IC redirects: 'add it as a hypothesis in the tech channel'

The IC posts a status update in the bridge channel every 10 minutes for P0/P1 and every 30 minutes for P2. The format is fixed to make scanning for information fast. Stakeholders should be able to read a status update in 20 seconds.
The Scribe copies each status update into the postmortem timeline with a timestamp. This produces the incident timeline automatically without requiring anyone to reconstruct it after the fact.
Escalation Procedures from the War Room

Escalation from inside the war room follows a different path than initial paging. The bridge is already active; the IC needs to add people or move to a higher severity routing, not start from scratch.

# Downgrade or upgrade severity (re-routes PagerDuty, updates channel topic,
# sends notification to relevant stakeholders automatically):
/ic set-severity p0 --reason "Safety classifier flagging prompt injection on 3% of requests"

# Bot actions on P1 -> P0 upgrade:
# 1. Updates bridge topic to [P0]
# 2. Creates #inc-{id}-exec if not already present
# 3. Pages VP Engineering and on-call legal/privacy lead
# 4. Posts to internal status dashboard
# 5. Starts 72-hour GDPR notification window if privacy-related

# Add a subject matter expert to the tech channel:
/ic add-sme @model-quality-oncall --context "Quality score drop on streaming completions"

# Bot:
# - Adds @model-quality-oncall to #inc-{id}-tech
# - Posts context message so SME sees the issue immediately
# - Notifies IC that SME has been added and their last-seen time

Any incident with a potential data or privacy dimension must loop in the Privacy team before any public statement is made and before any affected data is accessed for investigation. The bot enforces this for P0 incidents by blocking the first external status page update until a Privacy acknowledgement is recorded.

# Required for P0 data/privacy incidents before status page update:
/ic privacy-ack --lead @privacy-oncall

# Bot unblocks status page publishing once acknowledged.
# Bot also starts the 72-hour GDPR Article 33 notification clock.

For P0 incidents affecting a physical deployment or requiring hardware intervention, a physical war room is stood up in addition to the virtual bridge. The following applies to Anthropic office locations; adapt for other deployment environments.
The IC bot books the designated incident room (Conf-Incident-1 at HQ) automatically for P0 incidents with duration 4 hours, extendable in 2-hour blocks.
Dashboard screen shows the primary alert metric and the Grafana incident dashboard. A second screen mirrors the bridge Slack channel.
All remote participants join the Zoom bridge regardless of physical room attendance. No one is IC via physical presence only.
For incidents > 3 hours, the IC designates 10-minute break rotations so at least one person is always watching the dashboards.
Used for the hypothesis board: three columns -- Confirmed / Investigating / Ruled Out. Photo taken every 30 minutes and posted to the tech channel.
After resolution, the IC runs the teardown sequence. Teardown is not the same as incident closure; closure is a severity change. Teardown is the administrative cleanup that follows.
to record official resolution time and update the status page to resolved.
Archive the bridge and sub-channels. Bot preserves messages for 90 days in the incident archive.
to convert the auto-generated postmortem draft to review-pending status.
If P0/P1: send the internal close notification. Bot drafts it; IC approves before sending.
Inconsistent naming; not linked to incident record; no bot integration
Always use /ic declare; block manual channel creation in incident guidelines
Skipping the bridge call for P1
Async-only coordination slows decisions by 3-5x during active degradation
Mandate bridge call for P0 and P1; make it optional for P2
Late joiners spend 5 min scrolling to find basic details
Run /ic pin-context within 2 min of declaration; bot reminds IC
All engineers posting in the bridge
Bridge becomes unusable; IC loses signal in noise
Enforce channel separation; route engineers to the tech channel
Delaying exec channel creation for P1
Executives find out through informal channels; loss of trust
Create #-exec at declaration for P0/P1; IC posts opening brief within 15 min
Timeline must be reconstructed from memory post-incident; postmortem accuracy suffers
Assign Scribe role explicitly; bot reminds IC if no Scribe assigned within 5 min
Automating War Room Setup with the Incident Bot

The IC bot integrates with PagerDuty, Slack, Zoom, Notion, and the internal status page. Understanding what the bot does automatically versus what requires IC action prevents the IC from duplicating bot work or missing steps the bot cannot handle.
Creates bridge, tech, comms channels; pages oncall SRE; creates postmortem draft; sets PagerDuty routing
Assign Tech Lead and Comms Lead within 2 min
Creates exec channel; pages VP Eng and on-call legal/privacy; starts GDPR clock if privacy flag set
Post exec opening brief within 15 min
Post status update using fixed template
Records resolution time; archives channels; converts postmortem to review-pending; sends close notification draft
Blocks publish until Privacy ack received

# incident-bot-config.yaml -- severity-based behaviour
severity_defaults:
  p0:
    channels: [bridge, tech, comms, exec]
    page_groups: [oncall-sre, vp-engineering, privacy-oncall]
    bridge_call: required
    status_update_interval_min: 10
    exec_brief_sla_min: 15
    gdpr_clock: conditional_on_privacy_flag
  p1:
    channels: [bridge, tech, comms, exec]
    page_groups: [oncall-sre, vp-engineering]
    bridge_call: required
    status_update_interval_min: 10
    exec_brief_sla_min: 30
  p2:
    channels: [bridge, tech, comms]
    page_groups: [oncall-sre]
    bridge_call: optional
    status_update_interval_min: 30
  p3:
    channels: [bridge, tech]
    page_groups: [oncall-sre]
    bridge_call: not_opened
    status_update_interval_min: 60
Bridge Call Recording and Evidence Preservation
All P0 and P1 bridge calls are recorded automatically when the IC runs
. Recordings and channel message exports are preserved as part of the incident record for postmortem analysis and, in P0 safety or privacy incidents, potential regulatory review.
Stored encrypted for 90 days in the incident archive. The Scribe pins the recording link in the bridge channel within 5 minutes of call end.
All channels in the #inc-{id}* namespace are exported at teardown and attached to the postmortem Notion page.
at declaration and at each severity change. The snapshot is attached to the postmortem and the incident channel.
For P0 incidents with a legal or regulatory dimension, the Privacy team places a hold on the channel export. The IC bot blocks the archive step until hold status is confirmed.
For physical war rooms, the Scribe photographs the whiteboard hypothesis board every 30 minutes and posts to the tech channel. Photos are included in the postmortem export.

Bridge call participants are notified of recording at the start of every call. The IC bot posts a recording-in-progress banner as a pinned message immediately when the call starts. Participants who have not consented to recording must leave and coordinate via Slack only.

def open_bridge_call(incident_id: str, ic_user_id: str) -> dict:
    """
    Opens the Zoom bridge, starts recording, and posts consent banner.
    Returns {zoom_url, recording_id, banner_message_ts}.
    """
    zoom_url = create_zoom_meeting(
        topic=f"Incident Bridge {incident_id}",
        auto_record="cloud",
        waiting_room=False,
    )
    recording_id = start_cloud_recording(zoom_url["meeting_id"])

    # Pin consent banner to bridge channel
    banner = post_to_channel(
        channel=f"inc-{incident_id}",
        text=(
            f":record_button: Bridge call recording in progress. "
            f"Recording ID: {recording_id}. "
            f"By joining the call you consent to recording. "
            f"Coordinate via Slack only if you do not consent."
        ),
        pin=True,
    )
    return {
        "zoom_url": zoom_url["join_url"],
        "recording_id": recording_id,
        "banner_message_ts": banner["ts"],
    }
War Room Metrics and Continuous Improvement
The incident bot collects timing metrics for every war room. These metrics are reviewed in the monthly reliability review and used to tune the setup process.
IC coaching; add reminder to bootstrap checklist
declare to first exec channel message
Reminder SLA baked into bot; alert if missed
Bot DMs IC; programme lead review if > 3 incidents miss

A war room setup that consistently misses the time-to-first-status-update target is usually a sign that the IC bootstrap checklist is being done mentally rather than procedurally. The fix is to require ICs to run the checklist from the pinned message, not from memory.

A war room is a structured information environment, not just a meeting room or a chat channel. The IC bot creates the standard channel set -- bridge, tech, comms, and exec (P0/P1 only) -- within seconds of declaration, eliminating manual setup errors. The IC bootstraps the war room in under two minutes: pin context, assign leads, post opening status, open the bridge call for P1/P0. Bridge calls operate under strict norms -- muted observers, findings in Slack not spoken aloud, no parallel side conversations -- so the IC can maintain situational awareness across all channels. Status updates follow a fixed template posted every 10 minutes, feeding the postmortem timeline automatically. Teardown runs the close command, archives channels, and sets post-closure monitoring before the IC leaves the bridge.
